---
title: "Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R"
date: "April 22, 2026"
date-modified: "last modified"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 4
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: false
    code-copy: true
    css: styles.css
    include-after-body: toc-tracker.html
    theme: cosmo
    page-layout: article
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  warning: false
  freeze: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    out.width: "80%"
    fig.align: "center"
    fig.width: 12
    fig.height: 7
    dpi: 120
    fig.show: animate
    fig.animate: "data-frames=80;data-fps=10"
---

::: welcome-section
# Welcome

This hands-on exercise explores how to bring data stories to life through animation. We move beyond static charts to create animated and interactive visualisations using **gganimate** and **plotly** — tools that let data evolve over time and invite audience engagement.
:::

---

## Overview {#overview}

When telling a visually-driven data story, animated graphics tends to attract the interest of the audience and make deeper impressions than static graphics. In this hands-on exercise, we learn how to create animated data visualisations by using **gganimate** and **plotly** R packages. We also learn how to:

- Reshape data using the **tidyr** package
- Process, wrangle and transform data using the **dplyr** package

### Basic Concepts of Animation {#basic-concepts}

When creating animations, the plot does not actually move. Instead, many individual plots are built and then stitched together as movie frames — just like an old-school flip book or cartoon. Each frame is a different plot when conveying motion, which is built using some relevant subset of the aggregate data. The subset drives the flow of the animation when stitched back together.

![Animation concept: individual frames stitched together](https://r4va.netlify.app/chap04/img/image1.jpg){fig-align="center" width="70%"}

### Terminology {#terminology}

Before diving into creating animated statistical graphs, it is important to understand the key concepts:

1. **Frame**: Each frame represents a different point in time or a different category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph are updated to reflect the new data.

2. **Animation Attributes**: Settings that control how the animation behaves — such as the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start from the current frame or from the beginning.

::: {.callout-tip}
## When Should You Animate?

Before making animated graphs, ask: *Does it make sense to go through the effort?*

- For **exploratory data analysis**: an animated graphic may not be worth the time investment.
- For **presentations**: a few well-placed animated graphics can help an audience connect with your topic remarkably better than static counterparts.
:::

---

## Getting Started {#getting-started}

### Loading the R Packages {#loading-packages}

The following R packages are used in this exercise:

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| **readxl** | Import Excel workbooks |
| **gifski** | Convert video frames to GIF animations with efficient cross-frame palettes |
| **gapminder** | Provides the `country_colors` colour scheme |
| **plotly** | Interactive statistical graphs with animation support |
| **gganimate** | Extend ggplot2 grammar to include animation |
| **tidyverse** | Data wrangling, transformation and static visualisation |

```{r}
#| label: load-packages
pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse, ggplot2, dplyr)
options(gganimate.renderer = magick_renderer())
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

Using `pacman::p_load()` is a cleaner alternative to repeated `library()` calls — it installs missing packages automatically and loads all listed packages in one line. This keeps the setup code concise and reproducible across different machines.
:::

---

### Importing the Data {#importing-data}

The *Data* worksheet from the *GlobalPopulation* Excel workbook is used throughout this exercise. The data captures population statistics (percentage aged, percentage young, total population) across countries and continents over multiple years.

**Evolution of the import code — from deprecated to modern dplyr:**

The original code used deprecated functions. The modern equivalent using `across()` is preferred:

```{r}
#| label: import-data
col <- c("Country", "Continent")

globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

- `read_xls()` from **readxl** handles Excel files natively without needing to save as CSV first.
- The shift from `mutate_each_()` + `funs()` → `mutate_at()` → `mutate(across())` reflects how the **dplyr** API matured toward a more consistent, expressive design. `across()` is the modern standard and works seamlessly inside any `mutate()` or `summarise()` call.
- Converting `Year` to integer (not double) is critical for `transition_time()` to treat it correctly as a discrete time variable.
:::

---

## Data Exploration {#data-exploration}

Before animating, it is essential to understand the structure and distribution of the data. Visual exploration helps us identify patterns, outliers and whether animation will add analytical value.

### Dataset Structure

```{r}
#| label: data-glimpse
glimpse(globalPop)
```

```{r}
#| label: data-summary
summary(globalPop)
```

### Distribution of Key Variables

```{r}
#| label: explore-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of % Aged, % Young, and Population across all years"

p1 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#3182bd", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of % Aged Population",
       x = "% Aged", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p2 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Young)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#e6550d", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of % Young Population",
       x = "% Young", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p3 <- ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Population / 1e6)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#31a354", colour = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Population Size",
       x = "Population (Millions)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

library(patchwork)
p1 | p2 | p3
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

The histograms immediately reveal that population size is highly right-skewed — a few very populous countries (e.g. China, India) dwarf the rest. This skew is why bubble charts use `scale_size(range = c(2, 12))` to compress the visual range and prevent tiny countries from becoming invisible.
:::

### Trend Over Time by Continent

```{r}
#| label: explore-trend-continent
#| fig-cap: "Mean % Aged and % Young by Continent across Years"

globalPop %>%
  group_by(Continent, Year) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_Old   = mean(Old,   na.rm = TRUE),
    Mean_Young = mean(Young, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Mean_Old, Mean_Young),
               names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(Metric = recode(Metric,
                         "Mean_Old"   = "% Aged",
                         "Mean_Young" = "% Young")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Value, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Demographic Shift by Continent Over Time",
       subtitle = "Mean % Aged rises as % Young falls — the global ageing trend",
       x = "Year", y = "Mean Percentage",
       colour = "Continent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

This faceted line chart confirms the **demographic transition** narrative that the animated bubble chart will later tell dynamically. Ageing populations rise consistently across all continents while the proportion of young people declines — animation will make this drift visually compelling in ways a static chart cannot.
:::

### Old vs Young: Scatter by Continent (Static Snapshot)

```{r}
#| label: explore-scatter-continent
#| fig-cap: "Old vs Young % coloured by Continent — all years combined"

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Old vs Young: All Countries, All Years",
       subtitle = "Strong negative correlation — as ageing rises, youth proportion falls",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young",
       colour = "Continent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

The clear negative correlation between % Aged and % Young across all data points (all countries, all years pooled) gives us strong motivation to animate: by splitting this cloud of points by Year, the animation will reveal how the entire distribution shifts toward higher ageing over decades — something impossible to perceive in a single static chart.
:::

### Number of Countries per Continent

```{r}
#| label: explore-country-count
#| fig-cap: "Number of countries per continent in the dataset"

globalPop %>%
  distinct(Country, Continent) %>%
  count(Continent, name = "n_countries") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Continent, n_countries), y = n_countries,
             fill = Continent)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_countries), hjust = -0.2, size = 4) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Dataset Coverage: Countries per Continent",
       x = "Continent", y = "Number of Countries") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  ylim(0, 60)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

Africa and Asia each have many countries in the dataset while Oceania has few. This imbalance is worth noting when interpreting the animated bubble plot — the bubbles for Africa will be densely clustered while Oceania may appear sparse. `country_colors` from **gapminder** ensures consistent colouring regardless of this count difference.
:::

---

## Animated Data Visualisation: gganimate Methods {#gganimate}

**gganimate** extends ggplot2's grammar of graphics to include animation. It does so through new grammar classes added to the plot object:

| Function family | Role |
|----------------|------|
| `transition_*()` | How data is spread across time |
| `view_*()` | How positional scales change during animation |
| `shadow_*()` | How data from other time points is shown |
| `enter_*()/exit_*()` | How new data appears / old data disappears |
| `ease_aes()` | Controls the pacing of aesthetic transitions |

### Building a Static Population Bubble Plot {#static-bubble}

Before animating, we first build the static version. This helps verify the visual design and aesthetics before adding motion.

```{r}
#| label: static-bubble
#| fig-cap: "Static bubble plot — all years overlaid (note the {frame_time} placeholder in title)"

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Static version — all years overlaid",
       x = "% Aged", 
       y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

The static plot with all years overlaid creates visual noise — overlapping bubbles make it impossible to distinguish temporal change. The `{frame_time}` placeholder in the title does not yet render because there is no `transition_time()` applied. This clearly shows *why* animation adds value: without it, the temporal narrative is completely hidden.

`scale_size(range = c(2, 12))` is critical — it maps the raw population values (which span orders of magnitude) to a visually sensible dot size range so small countries remain visible.
:::

---

### Building the Animated Bubble Plot {#animated-bubble-gganimate}

Now we add the gganimate layer. `transition_time(Year)` slices the data by Year and renders one frame per year, creating the impression of temporal movement.

```{r}
#| label: animated-bubble-gganimate
#| fig-cap: "Animated bubble chart using gganimate — countries shift rightward (ageing) over time"
#| cache: true

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Each bubble = one country. Size = Population.",
       x = "% Aged", 
       y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

- `transition_time(Year)` is the core animation function. It automatically creates one frame per unique Year value and interpolates bubble positions between frames using the easing function.
- `ease_aes("linear")` makes each aesthetic change at a constant rate across the transition. Alternative easing methods (quadratic, elastic, bounce, etc.) create different "feel" — linear is most honest for showing demographic data where we want precise temporal mapping.
- The animated GIF makes the rightward drift (increasing % Aged) and downward drift (decreasing % Young) immediately obvious — the global demographic transition becomes a visual narrative rather than a table of numbers.
- `show.legend = FALSE` works correctly in gganimate (unlike in plotly, as we will see).
:::

#### Deeper Exploration: shadow_wake() for Trajectory Trails

`shadow_wake()` leaves a fading "wake" behind each bubble, making individual country trajectories visible even during the animation.

```{r}
#| label: shadow-wake
#| fig-cap: "Adding shadow_wake() to reveal country trajectories during animation"
#| cache: true

ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}  |  Trails show historical trajectory",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = 0.2, alpha = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

`shadow_wake()` is a powerful addition when you want to show *where a data point has been*, not just where it is now. The `wake_length` parameter controls how much historical trail is shown (0 = none, 1 = full history). This is especially useful for demographic bubbles because it reveals which countries are ageing rapidly (long rightward trails) versus those that are more stable — analytical insight that would otherwise require a separate panel.
:::

---

## Animated Data Visualisation: plotly {#plotly}

**plotly** offers a different approach to animation: instead of rendering individual frames into a GIF, it creates interactive HTML animations. Both `ggplotly()` and `plot_ly()` support key-frame animations through the `frame` argument/aesthetic. They also support an `ids` argument/aesthetic to ensure smooth transitions between objects with the same id — facilitating **object constancy** (a key principle of good animation design).

### Building an Animated Bubble Plot: `ggplotly()` Method {#ggplotly-method}

#### First Attempt — Legend Issue

`ggplotly()` converts a ggplot2 object into an interactive plotly widget. Note the known issue with `show.legend = FALSE`:

```{r}
#| label: ggplotly-v1
#| fig-cap: "ggplotly animation — note legend still appears despite show.legend = FALSE"

gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
             aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                 size = Population, 
                 colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young")

ggplotly(gg)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

`show.legend = FALSE` inside `geom_point()` does **not** suppress the legend in `ggplotly()`. This is because `ggplotly()` translates each colour mapping to a separate plotly trace, and trace visibility is controlled separately from ggplot2's legend logic. The fix is to use ggplot2's `theme(legend.position = 'none')` at the plot level — which plotly does respect during conversion.

This is a good reminder that `ggplotly()` is a *translation layer*, not a perfect mirror. Some ggplot2 arguments behave differently once converted.
:::

#### Fixed Version — Using `theme(legend.position = 'none')`

```{r}
#| label: ggplotly-v2
#| fig-cap: "ggplotly animation — legend correctly suppressed using theme()"

library(plotly)

gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
             aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                 size = Population, 
                 colour = Country,
                 frame = Year)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young")

ggplotly(gg) %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

The animated bubble plot rendered by `ggplotly()` includes a **play/pause button** and a **year slider** — two interactive controls that the gganimate GIF cannot provide. This interactivity fundamentally changes the viewer experience:

- The **play/pause** allows a presenter to stop at a specific year to discuss it in detail.
- The **slider** allows viewers to manually scrub through time — enabling personalised exploration.
- **Hover tooltips** show exact values for each country's bubble on demand.

This is the key trade-off: `gganimate` produces a self-running GIF ideal for embedding in reports or social media, while `plotly` produces an interactive widget ideal for web dashboards and exploratory analysis.
:::

---

### Building an Animated Bubble Plot: `plot_ly()` Method {#plotly-method}

`plot_ly()` provides more direct control over plotly's native API. Here we colour by **Continent** rather than Country, making continental patterns easier to distinguish.

```{r}
#| label: plotly-native
#| fig-cap: "plot_ly() animated bubble chart — coloured by Continent, hover shows country name"

bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year, 
          text       = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE,
         xaxis = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis = list(title = "% Young"),
         title = list(text = "Global Demographics Over Time"))
bp
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

`plot_ly()` gives finer control than `ggplotly()` for several reasons:

- **`sizes = c(2, 100)`** maps the population range to plotly's native size scale (different units from ggplot2's `scale_size()`).
- **`hoverinfo = "text"` + `text = ~Country`** gives clean, custom tooltips showing only the country name.
- **`color = ~Continent`** (continent-level colouring) is analytically superior to country-level colouring for spotting continental clusters — Africa's young/low-aged cluster vs Europe's aged/low-young cluster become immediately clear.
- `frame = ~Year` is the native plotly equivalent of `transition_time(Year)` in gganimate — but plotly renders this as a JavaScript-driven interactive animation, not a pre-rendered GIF.
:::

#### Deeper Exploration: `plot_ly()` with `ids` for Object Constancy

Adding `ids = ~Country` ensures that each bubble maintains identity across frames, enabling smooth interpolation (object constancy):

```{r}
#| label: plotly-ids
#| fig-cap: "plot_ly() with ids for smooth object constancy across animation frames"

bp_ids <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year,
          ids        = ~Country,
          text       = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis      = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis      = list(title = "% Young"),
         title      = list(text = "Global Demographics — Smooth Transitions (ids = Country)"),
         legend     = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_ids
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

**Object constancy** is a fundamental principle of animated visualisation design: viewers should be able to *follow* a specific object as it moves across frames. Without `ids`, plotly may re-match bubbles arbitrarily between frames, causing confusing "teleportation" of bubbles. Adding `ids = ~Country` tells plotly which bubble in frame N corresponds to which bubble in frame N+1, enabling smooth, continuous motion for each country. This makes individual country trajectories trackable by eye — a critical advantage for analytical storytelling.
:::

#### Deeper Exploration: Animated Bubble Chart with Population Labels for Key Countries

For an enhanced presentation-ready version, we highlight the most populous countries with labels:

```{r}
#| label: plotly-enhanced
#| fig-cap: "Enhanced plot_ly() — top 5 most populous countries labelled for tracking"

# Identify top 5 countries by max population for labelling
top_countries <- globalPop %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  summarise(max_pop = max(Population, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(max_pop, n = 5) %>%
  pull(Country)

globalPop_enhanced <- globalPop %>%
  mutate(Label = if_else(Country %in% top_countries, as.character(Country), ""))

bp_enhanced <- globalPop_enhanced %>%
  plot_ly(x          = ~Old, 
          y          = ~Young, 
          size       = ~Population, 
          color      = ~Continent,
          sizes      = c(2, 100),
          frame      = ~Year,
          ids        = ~Country,
          text       = ~paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>",
                               "Population: ", scales::comma(Population), "<br>",
                               "% Aged: ", round(Old, 1), "%<br>",
                               "% Young: ", round(Young, 1), "%"),
          hoverinfo  = "text",
          type       = "scatter",
          mode       = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis      = list(title = "% Aged Population"),
         yaxis      = list(title = "% Young Population"),
         title      = list(text = "Global Demographic Transition Over Time"),
         legend     = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_enhanced
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What I Learned

Combining `ids` with rich hover text (`paste0()` for multi-line tooltips) creates a presentation-quality chart. The custom tooltip reveals Population, % Aged, and % Young simultaneously on hover — providing detailed information without cluttering the visual. This illustrates the key analytical advantage of interactive plotly animations over static charts: viewers can dive deeper into specific data points of interest without the analyst needing to pre-decide what to highlight.
:::

---

## Comparison: gganimate vs plotly {#comparison}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### gganimate Summary

**Best for:**

- Embedding animated GIFs in static reports, PDFs, or presentations
- Self-running animations in social media or email
- Full ggplot2 integration (all geoms, themes, scales work natively)
- Offline use

**Key functions used:**

- `transition_time()` — temporal animation
- `ease_aes()` — controls transition pacing
- `shadow_wake()` — trajectory trails

### plotly Summary

**Best for:**

- Interactive web dashboards
- Exploratory analysis where viewers want to pause, hover and explore
- Play/pause + slider controls for presentation
- Richer tooltip information on demand

**Key functions used:**

- `ggplotly()` — converts ggplot2 objects (watch for legend quirks)
- `plot_ly()` — native plotly API with more control
- `frame = ~Year` — keyframe animation
- `ids = ~Country` — object constancy for smooth transitions

### Side-by-Side Design Decision Guide

| Criterion | gganimate | plotly |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Output format | GIF / video | Interactive HTML widget |
| Viewer interactivity | None (auto-plays) | Play/pause, slider, hover |
| ggplot2 compatibility | Full | Partial (via `ggplotly()`) |
| Object constancy | Built-in via `transition_time()` | Requires `ids =` argument |
| Best context | Reports, slides, social | Dashboards, web, Shiny |
| File size | Small GIF | Larger HTML |

:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Overall What I Learned

The most important insight from this exercise is that animation is not just decoration — it is a **narrative tool**. The demographic transition (ageing populations, declining youth proportions) is a well-documented phenomenon, but seeing it unfold year by year through an animated bubble chart makes it viscerally real in a way that a data table or even a static scatter plot cannot.

The choice between **gganimate** and **plotly** is a choice between two kinds of stories:
- gganimate tells a *directed* story — you show the audience what you want them to see, at the pace you choose.
- plotly invites *exploratory* storytelling — the audience can ask their own questions by hovering, pausing and scrubbing through time.

Both are valuable. The key skill is knowing which one serves your audience and context best.
:::

---

# References

## ggiraph

- [ggiraph package documentation](https://davidgohel.github.io/ggiraph/index.html) — online reference guide and articles
- [Customizing girafe objects](https://www.ardata.fr/ggiraph-book/customize.html) — CSS options for tooltip, hover, and selection
- [ggiraph Book — Selections](https://www.ardata.fr/ggiraph-book/selections.html) — data_id, Shiny integration

## plotly for R

- [Getting Started with Plotly in R](https://plotly.com/r/getting-started/)
- Carson Sievert (2020) *Interactive web-based data visualization with R, plotly, and shiny*, Chapman and Hall/CRC — [online version](https://plotly-r.com/)
- [Plotly R Figure Reference](https://plotly.com/r/reference/index/) — comprehensive visual reference
- [Plotly R Library Fundamentals](https://plotly.com/r/plotly-fundamentals/)

## crosstalk & DT

- [crosstalk documentation](https://rstudio.github.io/crosstalk/)
- [DT package guide](https://rstudio.github.io/DT/)

## Source

- [R for Visual Analytics Chapter 3](https://r4va.netlify.app/chap03)
